questions.question:
Let P(S) denote the power set of set S. Which of the following is always true?

A. P(S) ∩ P(T) ⊆ P(S ∩ T)
B. P(S) ∩ P(T) = P(S ∩ T)
C. P(S) ∩ P(T) ⊃ P(S ∩ T)
D. P(S) ∩ P(T) - P(S ∩ T) = {Ø}
questions.to_find: which relationship between P(S) ∩ P(T) and P(S ∩ T) always holds

understand.plain: The power set P(X) is the collection of all subsets of X. A set A belongs to P(S) ∩ P(T) if and only if A ⊆ S and A ⊆ T, which is equivalent to A ⊆ S ∩ T, which means A ∈ P(S ∩ T). So both inclusions hold simultaneously, making the two sets equal.

keywords.explain: The set of all subsets of S, including the empty set and S itself; has 2^|S| elements.
P(S) ∩ P(T) contains exactly those sets that are subsets of both S and T, i.e., subsets of S ∩ T.

understand.visual_alt: A is in P(S) ∩ P(T) iff A ⊆ S and A ⊆ T iff A ⊆ S ∩ T iff A is in P(S ∩ T), showing the equality holds for any sets S and T.

given.aim: Prove which of the four relations between P(S) ∩ P(T) and P(S ∩ T) is always true.

terms.meaning: power set of S — the collection of all subsets of S
sets that are simultaneously subsets of S and subsets of T
terms.example: A ∈ P(S) ∩ P(T) iff A ⊆ S and A ⊆ T
terms.connects: relates to subsets of S ∩ T

terms.meaning: power set of the intersection — all subsets of S ∩ T
terms.example: A ∈ P(S ∩ T) iff A ⊆ S ∩ T
terms.connects: equivalent condition to A ⊆ S and A ⊆ T

given.plan: Show both P(S) ∩ P(T) ⊆ P(S ∩ T) and P(S ∩ T) ⊆ P(S) ∩ P(T) using element-chasing; conclude equality.

steps.title: Forward inclusion: P(S) ∩ P(T) ⊆ P(S ∩ T)
steps.formula_raw: A ∈ P(S) ∩ P(T) => A ⊆ S and A ⊆ T => A ⊆ S ∩ T => A ∈ P(S ∩ T)
steps.apply: Let A ∈ P(S) ∩ P(T). Then A ⊆ S and A ⊆ T. Every element of A is in both S and T, so every element of A is in S ∩ T, giving A ⊆ S ∩ T, i.e., A ∈ P(S ∩ T).
steps.note: This direction holds by the definition of intersection.

steps.title: Backward inclusion: P(S ∩ T) ⊆ P(S) ∩ P(T)
steps.formula_raw: A ∈ P(S ∩ T) => A ⊆ S ∩ T => A ⊆ S and A ⊆ T => A ∈ P(S) ∩ P(T)
steps.apply: Let A ∈ P(S ∩ T). Then A ⊆ S ∩ T. Since S ∩ T ⊆ S, we get A ⊆ S; similarly A ⊆ T. So A ∈ P(S) and A ∈ P(T), giving A ∈ P(S) ∩ P(T).

solution.result: P(S) ∩ P(T) = P(S ∩ T) always — option B.

questions.formula_note: The element-chasing argument hinges on the equivalent characterisations A ⊆ S and A ⊆ T iff A ⊆ S ∩ T, which is the core identity behind power-set intersection.

questions.question:
Let S be a set of n elements {1, 2, ..., n} and G a graph with 2^n vertices, each vertex corresponding to a distinct subset of S. Two vertices are adjacent iff the symmetric difference of the corresponding subsets has exactly 2 elements.

Note: The symmetric difference of two sets A_1 and A_2 is defined as (A_1 - A_2) ∪ (A_2 - A_1).

Every vertex in G has the same degree. How many connected components does G have?
questions.answer: Every vertex has degree C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2, and G has 2 connected components (even-cardinality subsets and odd-cardinality subsets form separate components).
questions.to_find: degree of each vertex and number of connected components of G

understand.plain: Two subsets are adjacent whenever their symmetric difference has exactly 2 elements, meaning they differ on exactly 2 positions in their binary characteristic vectors. Any two-element subset T of S can be toggled (elements in T are added if absent, removed if present), giving C(n,2) neighbours for every vertex. Toggling 2 elements changes the cardinality by 0, +2, or -2, so parity is preserved — even-cardinality and odd-cardinality subsets never mix, giving exactly 2 connected components.

keywords.explain: A △ B = (A - B) ∪ (B - A) — elements in exactly one of the two sets; |A △ B| = 2 means they differ in exactly 2 positions.
Toggling any 2-element set changes cardinality by -2, 0, or +2, so the parity (even/odd) of the subset size is invariant along edges.
keywords.example: Starting from {1,3} (size 2, even) all reachable subsets also have even size.

keywords.explain: Each of the C(n,2) two-element subsets of S defines exactly one neighbour obtained by toggling those elements; this count is the same for every vertex.

understand.visual_alt: Each vertex (subset of S) connects to exactly C(n,2) others obtained by toggling any 2-element subset of S; the graph splits into even-parity and odd-parity halves.

given.aim: Find the common degree and the number of connected components of the symmetric-difference graph on all subsets of an n-element set.

terms.meaning: A and B differ on exactly 2 elements of S
terms.example: {1,2} and {1,3} differ on {2,3}, so they are adjacent

terms.meaning: whether the subset has an even or odd number of elements; toggling 2 elements never changes parity
terms.example: from a size-3 subset, all neighbours have size 1, 3, or 5 — always odd

given.plan: Count neighbours of an arbitrary vertex A by counting 2-element subsets T ⊆ S to toggle; use parity invariance to identify the 2 connected components.

steps.title: Compute the degree of a vertex A
steps.apply: The subsets B with |A △ B| = 2 are exactly the subsets obtained by choosing any 2-element subset T of S and setting B = A △ T. There are C(n,2) choices for T, each giving a distinct B. So deg(A) = C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2 for every vertex A.
steps.note: This count is independent of A, confirming all degrees are equal.

steps.title: Determine connected components via parity
steps.apply: Since each edge toggles exactly 2 elements, the parity of the subset size (even or odd) is preserved along every edge. The 2^(n-1) subsets of even cardinality form one component and the 2^(n-1) subsets of odd cardinality form the other (reachability within each parity class can be verified). Hence G has exactly 2 connected components.
steps.note: For n >= 2, both components are non-trivial and connected within themselves.

solution.result: Every vertex has degree C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2; G has 2 connected components.

questions.formula_note: C(n,2) counts the number of 2-element subsets of S, which equals the degree of each vertex; the parity invariance of toggling 2 elements explains the 2-component structure.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

S_1: There exist infinite sets A, B, C such that A ∩ (B ∪ C) is finite.
S_2: There exist two irrational numbers x and y such that (x + y) is rational.

Which of the following is true about S_1 and S_2?

A. Only S_1 is correct
B. Only S_2 is correct
C. Both S_1 and S_2 are correct
D. None of S_1 and S_2 is correct
questions.answer: C. Both S_1 and S_2 are correct
questions.to_find: which of the two existence statements S_1 and S_2 are true

understand.plain: For S_1, you can find infinite sets where the intersection happens to be finite — a simple counterexample: let A = natural numbers N, B = even numbers, C = odd numbers; then B ∪ C = N, so A ∩ (B ∪ C) = N which is infinite. But try A = {0}, B = N, C = N; that's finite A. Actually, A=N, B={0}, C={0} makes A ∩ (B ∪ C) = {0}, finite, even though A, B, C are all infinite. For S_2, x = sqrt(2) and y = -sqrt(2) are both irrational but x + y = 0 is rational.

keywords.explain: A statement of the form 'there exist...' is true as long as you can find one valid example (a witness).
keywords.example: S_2 is proved true by x = sqrt(2), y = -sqrt(2).

keywords.explain: A real number that cannot be expressed as a ratio of two integers; examples: sqrt(2), pi, e.
keywords.example: sqrt(2) is irrational but sqrt(2) + (-sqrt(2)) = 0, which is rational.

understand.visual_alt: S_1 is witnessed by A = N, B = {0}, C = {1}: all infinite but A ∩ (B ∪ C) = {0,1} is finite. S_2 is witnessed by sqrt(2) and -sqrt(2) whose sum is 0.

given.aim: Verify whether each of S_1 and S_2 holds by producing a witness example.

terms.meaning: existence of infinite A, B, C with A ∩ (B ∪ C) finite
terms.connects: requires A to share only finitely many elements with B ∪ C

terms.meaning: existence of irrationals x, y whose sum is rational
terms.example: x = sqrt(2), y = -sqrt(2) are irrational, x + y = 0 ∈ Q
terms.connects: additive inverse of an irrational is irrational

terms.meaning: a single concrete instance that confirms an existence claim
terms.example: one valid (A, B, C) triple is enough to verify S_1
terms.connects: existence proofs require only one example

given.plan: Construct explicit witnesses for both S_1 and S_2; if both succeed, the answer is C.

steps.title: Verify S_1 with a concrete example
steps.apply: Let A = N (all natural numbers), B = {0}, C = {1}. All three sets are infinite (N has infinitely many elements; single-element sets are finite — wait, we need B and C infinite too). Correct witness: A = N, B = even numbers, C = {1}. Then B ∪ C contains 1 and all evens. A ∩ (B ∪ C) = evens ∪ {1} — still infinite. Better: A = N, B = {2, 4, 6, ...}, C = {3} is not fully infinite for C. Use A = N, B = N, C = N; A ∩ (B ∪ C) = N (infinite). Instead: let A = {2, 4, 6, ...}, B = {1, 2, 3}, C = {5, 6, 7, ...} (both infinite). A ∩ (B ∪ C) = {2} ∪ {6,8,10,...} still infinite. Simplest valid witness: A = {1,2,3,...} = N, B = {-1,-2,-3,...}, C = {-1,-2,-3,...}: A ∩ (B ∪ C) = empty set (finite!), and A, B, C are all infinite. So S_1 is TRUE.
steps.note: The key insight is that B ∪ C can be made disjoint from A even when all three sets are infinite.

steps.title: Verify S_2 with a concrete example
steps.formula_raw: x = sqrt(2), y = -sqrt(2)  =>  x + y = 0 ∈ Q
steps.apply: Both sqrt(2) and -sqrt(2) are irrational (if -sqrt(2) = p/q then sqrt(2) = -p/q, rational — contradiction). Their sum is sqrt(2) + (-sqrt(2)) = 0, which is rational. So S_2 is TRUE.
steps.note: Any irrational x paired with -x gives an irrational pair whose sum is 0.

solution.result: Both S_1 and S_2 are correct — answer C.

questions.formula_note: Both are existence statements proved by single witnesses; S_2 uses the fact that the additive inverse of an irrational is also irrational, so x + (-x) = 0 is rational.

questions.question:
a. Prove that powerset(A ∩ B) = powerset(A) ∩ powerset(B).

b. Let aperm: N x N -> N, where aperm(m, n) = min(m, n) for all natural numbers m and n. Using induction, prove that max(m, n) + min(m, n) = m + n for all natural numbers m and n.
questions.answer: Part a: P(A ∩ B) = P(A) ∩ P(B) because X ⊆ A ∩ B iff X ⊆ A and X ⊆ B (element-chasing in both directions). Part b: By induction on m (base: m=0 trivial; step: max(m+1,n)+min(m+1,n) = (m+1)+n by case analysis) — the identity max(m,n)+min(m,n) = m+n holds for all m,n ∈ N.
questions.to_find: proofs of (a) powerset intersection identity and (b) max + min = sum identity by induction

understand.plain: Part (a) is an element-chasing equality: a set X is a subset of A ∩ B exactly when it is a subset of A and also a subset of B, which is the definition of belonging to P(A) ∩ P(B). Part (b) is a combinatorial identity: for any two numbers, the larger plus the smaller equals their sum — essentially a tautology provable directly, or by induction by handling the two cases max = m or max = n at each step.

keywords.explain: A proof technique for set equality: show A ⊆ B and B ⊆ A by tracking an arbitrary element through the membership conditions.
keywords.example: X ∈ P(A∩B) means X ⊆ A∩B, which means X ⊆ A AND X ⊆ B.

keywords.explain: For any two numbers, max(m,n) picks the larger and min(m,n) picks the smaller; together they account for both numbers exactly once, so their sum equals m + n.

understand.visual_alt: Powerset intersection identity: membership in P(A) ∩ P(B) and membership in P(A∩B) are the same condition. Max-min identity: sorting a pair and summing is the same as summing unsorted.

given.aim: Prove the powerset-intersection identity by element chasing, and prove the max+min identity by mathematical induction.

terms.connects: equal to P(A) ∩ P(B) by element chasing

terms.meaning: sets that are subsets of both A and B simultaneously
the sum of the larger and smaller of two natural numbers
terms.connects: always equals m + n by definition of max and min

given.plan: For part (a) use bidirectional subset containment. For part (b) use induction on m: base case m=0, then for m+1 split into subcases n <= m+1 and n > m+1.

steps.title: Prove P(A ∩ B) = P(A) ∩ P(B) by element chasing
steps.formula_raw: X ⊆ A ∩ B iff (X ⊆ A and X ⊆ B)
steps.apply: (⊆) Let X ∈ P(A∩B). Then X ⊆ A∩B. For any x ∈ X, x ∈ A and x ∈ B, so X ⊆ A and X ⊆ B, giving X ∈ P(A) and X ∈ P(B), hence X ∈ P(A)∩P(B). (⊇) Let X ∈ P(A)∩P(B). Then X ⊆ A and X ⊆ B. Every x ∈ X satisfies x ∈ A and x ∈ B, so x ∈ A∩B, meaning X ⊆ A∩B, thus X ∈ P(A∩B). Both inclusions give equality.
steps.note: This is the same argument as 4.13.9 generalised and stated as a formal proof.

steps.title: Prove max(m,n) + min(m,n) = m + n by induction on m
steps.formula_raw: P(0) holds; P(m) => P(m+1) for all m; therefore P(m) for all m
steps.apply: Base: m=0. max(0,n)=n, min(0,n)=0, so max+min=n+0=n=0+n. True. Step: Assume max(m,n)+min(m,n)=m+n for all n. Consider m+1 and any n. Case 1: n <= m+1. Then max(m+1,n)=m+1, min(m+1,n)=n, sum=(m+1)+n. Case 2: n > m+1. Then max(m+1,n)=n, min(m+1,n)=m+1, sum=n+(m+1). Both equal (m+1)+n. QED.
steps.note: The identity is almost definitionally true since max and min partition the two values.

solution.result: Both proofs are complete; the identities P(A∩B) = P(A)∩P(B) and max(m,n)+min(m,n) = m+n hold for all sets A, B and all natural numbers m, n.

questions.formula_note: The powerset-intersection identity is the key result; it follows directly from the equivalence X ⊆ A ∩ B iff X ⊆ A and X ⊆ B, which is the defining property of set intersection.

questions.question:
Let A, B and C be non-empty sets and let X = (A - B) - C and Y = (A - C) - (B - C). Which one of the following is TRUE?

A. X = Y
B. X ⊂ Y
C. Y ⊂ X
D. None of these

understand.plain: Both X and Y describe elements that are in A but in neither B nor C. X removes B first and then removes C; Y removes C first and then removes (B minus C). In both cases an element qualifies iff it is in A, not in B, and not in C — so the conditions are identical and the sets are equal.

keywords.explain: Elements in A that are not in B; also written A \ B.
To test if two set expressions are equal, check membership: write out exactly when an element x is in each set using AND/NOT logic.
keywords.example: x ∈ (A-B)-C iff x ∈ A and x ∉ B and x ∉ C.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='200' class='bg' rx='10'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='100' rx='90' ry='70' fill='#CECBF6' opacity='0.5' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='180' cy='80' rx='70' ry='55' fill='#F1EFE8' opacity='0.7' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='185' cy='130' rx='65' ry='45' fill='#F1EFE8' opacity='0.7' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='55' y='105' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='215' y='65' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='218' y='155' class='lbl ink'>C</text><text x='95' y='150' class='sub ink'>X = Y</text><text x='75' y='170' class='sub mut'>(in A, not in B, not in C)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing region in A outside both B and C — this region is exactly X = (A-B)-C and also Y = (A-C)-(B-C).

given.aim: Show that (A-B)-C and (A-C)-(B-C) describe the same set by expanding membership conditions.

terms.meaning: elements in A, not in B, and not in C
terms.example: x ∈ X iff x ∈ A and x ∉ B and x ∉ C

terms.meaning: elements in (A but not C) that are not in (B but not C)
terms.example: x ∈ Y iff x ∈ A and x ∉ C and not(x ∈ B and x ∉ C)
terms.connects: simplifies to x ∈ A and x ∉ B and x ∉ C

terms.example: removes only elements that are in B but not in C

given.plan: Expand x ∈ X and x ∈ Y into logical conditions and show they are identical.

steps.title: Expand membership condition for X
steps.formula_raw: x ∈ (A-B)-C iff x ∈ A and x ∉ B and x ∉ C
steps.apply: x ∈ X = (A-B)-C iff x ∈ (A-B) and x ∉ C iff (x ∈ A and x ∉ B) and x ∉ C iff x ∈ A and x ∉ B and x ∉ C.

steps.title: Expand membership condition for Y and compare
steps.formula_raw: x ∈ (A-C)-(B-C) iff x ∈ A and x ∉ C and NOT(x ∈ B and x ∉ C)
steps.apply: x ∈ Y = (A-C)-(B-C) iff x ∈ (A-C) and x ∉ (B-C) iff (x ∈ A and x ∉ C) and not(x ∈ B and x ∉ C). The last part: not(x ∈ B and x ∉ C) = (x ∉ B) or (x ∈ C). Combined with x ∉ C: (x ∉ B or x ∈ C) and x ∉ C = x ∉ B and x ∉ C. So x ∈ Y iff x ∈ A and x ∉ B and x ∉ C. This is identical to x ∈ X. Hence X = Y.
steps.note: The simplification uses (P or Q) and (not Q) = P and (not Q).

questions.formula_note: Element-chasing (logical expansion of membership) is the key technique; both expressions reduce to x ∈ A and x ∉ B and x ∉ C, proving equality directly.

questions.question:
If P, Q, R are subsets of the universal set U, then (P ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ (P^c ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ Q^c ∪ R^c equals:

A. P^c ∪ Q^c ∪ R^c
B. P ∩ (Q ∪ R)
C. U
D. P ∪ (Q ∩ R)
questions.to_find: the simplification of (P ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ (P^c ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ Q^c ∪ R^c

understand.plain: Group the first two terms: (P ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ (P^c ∩ Q ∩ R) = (P ∪ P^c) ∩ Q ∩ R = U ∩ Q ∩ R = Q ∩ R. Then: (Q ∩ R) ∪ Q^c ∪ R^c. By De Morgan's law Q^c ∪ R^c = (Q ∩ R)^c. So the expression becomes (Q ∩ R) ∪ (Q ∩ R)^c = U.

keywords.explain: Every element is either in A or in A^c; together they cover the universal set.
keywords.example: (P ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ (P^c ∩ Q ∩ R) = (P ∪ P^c) ∩ Q ∩ R = Q ∩ R

keywords.explain: Q^c ∪ R^c = (Q ∩ R)^c — the complement of an intersection equals the union of complements.
keywords.example: Q^c ∪ R^c contains every element not in Q or not in R, i.e., not in Q ∩ R.

understand.visual_alt: Step 1 collapses the first two terms to Q ∩ R using P ∪ P^c = U. Step 2 the remaining union (Q ∩ R) ∪ (Q ∩ R)^c = U by the complementation law.

given.aim: Simplify the set expression (P ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ (P^c ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ Q^c ∪ R^c to a standard form.

terms.meaning: complement of P with respect to U — all elements in U not in P
A ∪ (B ∩ C) = (A ∪ B) ∩ (A ∪ C) and A ∩ (B ∪ C) = (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C)
terms.example: (P ∪ P^c) ∩ Q ∩ R = U ∩ Q ∩ R = Q ∩ R
terms.connects: used to factor out Q ∩ R

terms.meaning: any set united with its complement covers the universal set

given.plan: Factor the first two terms using distributivity, apply P ∪ P^c = U, then apply De Morgan and the complement law to get U.

steps.title: Simplify the first two terms
steps.formula_raw: (P ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ (P^c ∩ Q ∩ R) = (P ∪ P^c) ∩ Q ∩ R = U ∩ Q ∩ R = Q ∩ R
steps.apply: Factor Q ∩ R from both terms: (P ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ (P^c ∩ Q ∩ R) = (P ∪ P^c) ∩ Q ∩ R. Since P ∪ P^c = U and U ∩ X = X for any X, this equals Q ∩ R.
steps.note: Distributivity lets us factor out Q ∩ R even though P and P^c appear on different sides.

steps.title: Apply De Morgan's law and complement
steps.formula_raw: Q^c ∪ R^c = (Q ∩ R)^c  =>  (Q ∩ R) ∪ (Q ∩ R)^c = U
steps.apply: The full expression is now (Q ∩ R) ∪ Q^c ∪ R^c = (Q ∩ R) ∪ (Q ∩ R)^c (by De Morgan: Q^c ∪ R^c = (Q ∩ R)^c). A set united with its complement always equals U.
steps.note: The result is independent of P entirely.

solution.result: (P ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ (P^c ∩ Q ∩ R) ∪ Q^c ∪ R^c = U — option C.

questions.formula_note: De Morgan's law Q^c ∪ R^c = (Q ∩ R)^c is the key step; after factoring out Q ∩ R using distributivity, the complement law A ∪ A^c = U completes the simplification.

formulas.name: Power Set Intersection Identity
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing the power set of an intersection with the intersection of power sets; they are always equal.

terms.means: power set of X — the collection of all subsets of X
set of elements belonging to both A and B

formulas.trap: The analogous identity for union is NOT true in general: P(A ∪ B) ≠ P(A) ∪ P(B).
formulas.reference: Standard set theory; GATE CSE 2000 Q2.
